..summary: Explore and explore-multi responses emit `relation_type` at the top level AND `parameters.relation` with the same value, producing redundant and potentially confusing response keys

evidence: In buildCoreExploreResponse: response.put("relation_type", relationType) sets the top-level field.
In the same method: paramsUsed.put("relation", relationType) places the identical string inside the parameters sub-map.

A client reading both keys receives the same value under two different paths (response.relation_type and response.parameters.relation), but with different names, which implies they might carry different meanings.
detail.suggestion: Remove the top-level `relation_type` field and rely solely on `parameters.relation`, or rename one to make the duplication intentional and document it. Choosing one canonical location eliminates the ambiguity.

..summary: QueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern requires both `lemma` and `bcqlPattern` even though lemma is already substituted into the pattern, leaking the TotalFrequency implementation detail to callers

evidence: Javadoc for executeSurfacePattern states: 'Both lemma and bcqlPattern are required even though lemma has already been substituted into bcqlPattern: lemma is used to look up the head-word's total corpus frequency for logDice scoring, while bcqlPattern drives the Lucene span search.'

The caller must construct and pass the same lemma value twice — once embedded in the pattern string and once as a standalone parameter — to satisfy an internal scoring concern that should not be visible at the interface boundary.
detail.suggestion: Encapsulate the frequency-lookup behind the interface by having executeSurfacePattern extract the head lemma from the labeled bcqlPattern (position 1:) internally, or introduce a SurfacePatternRequest value object that carries both pieces of data together and documents the relationship explicitly. Alternatively, split the concern: add a separate getHeadFrequency call that the implementation makes internally so callers need not supply lemma twice.

..summary: Lemma extracted from URL path segment has no length limit, unlike all query parameters which are capped at MAX_PARAM_LENGTH=200

evidence: SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest(): `String lemma = parts[0];` — only checks isEmpty(), no length cap before passing lemma to query execution
HttpApiUtils.requireParam() enforces `MAX_PARAM_LENGTH = 200` and throws IllegalArgumentException for all query-string parameters used across ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers
RelationPatternBuilder.buildFullPattern() embeds the raw lemma string directly into a BCQL pattern via CqlUtils.substituteAtPosition() with no downstream length guard
detail.suggestion: Add a length validation for the path-segment lemma immediately after extraction in routeSketchRequest: `if (lemma.length() > HttpApiUtils.MAX_PARAM_LENGTH) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Lemma too long");` — this reuses the existing constant and wrapWithErrorHandling maps it to 400.

..summary: ConcordanceResult interface uses get-prefixed accessor names, violating the documented un-prefixed convention and forcing boilerplate bridge methods onto implementing records

evidence: ConcordanceResult interface declares getSentence(), getStartOffset(), getEndOffset(), getDocId()
model/package-info.java explicitly states: 'Record types omit the get prefix per Java record conventions. Traditional classes (non-records) also use un-prefixed accessors for consistency.'

SnippetResult and CollocateResult records each implement four boilerplate bridge methods solely to satisfy the interface (e.g. @Override public String getSentence() { return sentence; }) that would be unnecessary if the interface used sentence(), startOffset(), endOffset(), docId()
detail.suggestion: Rename the ConcordanceResult interface methods to un-prefixed forms (sentence(), startOffset(), endOffset(), docId()) to match the documented codebase convention; remove the now-redundant bridge override methods from SnippetResult and CollocateResult, since Java records auto-generate the matching un-prefixed accessor methods

..summary: GrammarConfig exposes get-prefixed accessor names inconsistent with the adjacent RelationConfig record's un-prefixed accessors, creating mixed calling style at every use site

evidence: GrammarConfig declares getRelations(), getRelation(String), getVersion(), getConfigPath()
RelationConfig (a record in the same package) uses id(), name(), description(), pattern(), relationType() — no get prefix

SketchHandlers.java line 81: for (var rel : grammarConfig.getRelations()) — then immediately accesses rel.id(), rel.name(), rel.pattern() without prefix, producing mixed calling style in the same loop body
model/package-info.java states un-prefixed accessors for both records and classes; GrammarConfig is the only non-record class in the production codebase that uses get-prefixed names
detail.suggestion: Rename GrammarConfig accessors from getRelations/getRelation/getVersion/getConfigPath to relations/relation/version/configPath to align with the documented accessor convention and eliminate the mixed-style calling pattern at use sites

..summary: GrammarConfigLoader.parse() lets JSONException escape unchecked, bypassing all callers' catch(IOException) guards

evidence: Line 97: JSONObject root = JSON.parseObject(content); — no try-catch; JSONException extends RuntimeException and is not declared in throws IOException

GrammarConfigLoader.load() declares throws IOException; callers (createDefaultEnglish, handleBlackLabServerCommand) catch only IOException and IllegalArgumentException — JSONException from a malformed grammar file escapes all three catch blocks

CorpusQueryHandlers line 70 and VisualizationHandlers line 39 both catch JSONException and re-throw as IllegalArgumentException, establishing the correct pattern for JSON parse boundaries that GrammarConfigLoader does not follow
createDefaultEnglish() claims in its Javadoc to provide an actionable IllegalStateException on malformed JSON but cannot deliver this when JSONException escapes unchecked
detail.suggestion: Wrap the JSON.parseObject(content) call in GrammarConfigLoader.parse() in a try-catch(JSONException) that rethrows as IOException: try { JSONObject root = JSON.parseObject(content); } catch (com.alibaba.fastjson2.JSONException e) { throw new IOException("Malformed grammar config JSON" + (configPath != null ? " at " + configPath : "") + ": " + e.getMessage(), e); }. This aligns with the pattern already used in CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers.

..summary: OVER_FETCH_FACTOR collects 3x hits but scoreHits truncates to maxResults before scoring, making the over-fetch a no-op

evidence: OVER_FETCH_FACTOR=3 constant comment: 'request 3x as many hits as needed to compensate for scoring discards'
executeBcqlQuery: sampleSize = (int) Math.min(hits.size(), (long) maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR) — collects up to 3*maxResults records
collectHits(hits, sampleSize, ...) gathers up to sampleSize (e.g. 30) HitRecords
scoreHits(hitRecords, ..., maxResults): limit = Math.min(records.size(), maxResults) — iterates only the first maxResults (e.g. 10) records, leaving the extra 20 collected records unscored
The sorted().limit(maxResults) in the caller operates on the already-truncated list, so the intended 'discard-and-re-rank' effect never fires
detail.suggestion: Remove the maxResults truncation from scoreHits so it processes all hitRecords: change 'int limit = Math.min(records.size(), maxResults)' to 'int limit = records.size()'. The caller's sorted().limit(maxResults) already enforces the final result cap. Alternatively, remove OVER_FETCH_FACTOR and sampleSize if over-fetching is not intended.

..summary: posMap.isEmpty() guard in buildAndRankCollocates is redundant — both branches of the ternary return the same value when the map is empty

evidence: Line: 'String pos = posMap.isEmpty() ? QueryResults.WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS : posMap.getOrDefault(collocateLemma, QueryResults.WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS);'
When posMap is empty, posMap.getOrDefault(collocateLemma, UNKNOWN_POS) returns UNKNOWN_POS — identical to the then-branch
The ternary simplifies to: posMap.getOrDefault(collocateLemma, UNKNOWN_POS)
detail.suggestion: Replace the ternary with: 'String pos = posMap.getOrDefault(collocateLemma, QueryResults.WordSketchResult.UNKNOWN_POS);'

..summary: collectFrequenciesFromGroups silently also collects POS tags but name only advertises frequency

evidence: Method signature: private void collectFrequenciesFromGroups(HitGroups groups, Function<String,String> lemmaExtractor, Map<String,Long> freqMap, Map<String,String> lemmaPosMap)
Javadoc says 'Iterates over HitGroups and populates frequency and POS maps' — the docs acknowledge the dual output but the method name does not.
Every call site passes both freqMap and lemmaPosMap and depends on both being populated after the call.
detail.suggestion: Rename to 'collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups' or 'extractLemmaFrequenciesAndPos' to make both outputs visible at the call site, eliminating the need to read the signature to discover the POS side effect.

..summary: fetchExamples parameters named 'adjective'/'noun' but the method is relation-agnostic

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.fetchExamples signature: public List<String> fetchExamples(@NonNull String adjective, @NonNull String noun, @NonNull RelationConfig relationConfig, ...) — Javadoc says '@param adjective The adjective lemma'.
The method accepts any RelationConfig including subject_of and object_of, where the first positional argument would be a verb lemma, not an adjective.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExamples: String adjective = HttpApiUtils.requireParam(params, "collocate"); — the HTTP param is named 'collocate' (relation-neutral) but the local variable is renamed to 'adjective', implying POS role.
The response echoes back 'collocate' (correct) while the internal logic used 'adjective' — a round-trip rename that signals the assumption was baked into the original API surface.
detail.suggestion: Rename the fetchExamples parameters to 'collocateLemma' and 'headLemma' (or 'seedLemma') to match the relation-neutral vocabulary used by the HTTP layer and the rest of the exploration API. Update the Javadoc accordingly. In handleSemanticFieldExamples, rename the local variables to match.
